blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنشَقَّتْ (١)
When the sky is split open (1)
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ (٢)
and gives ear to its Lord as it should. (2)
وَإِذَا ٱلْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ (٣)
When the earth is spread out (3)
وَأَلْقَتْ مَا فِيهَا وَتَخَلَّتْ (٤)
and throws out what is in it, emptying itself, (4)
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ (٥)
and gives ear to its Lord as it should. (5)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَـٰقِيهِ (٦)
O man! You are labouring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him. (6)
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ (٧)
Then, as for him who is given his record [of deeds] in his right hand, (7)
فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا (٨)
he will receive an easy reckoning, (8)
وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا (٩)
and he will return to his folks joyfully. (9)

The Content of Surah Al-Inshiqaq and the Virtue of Its Recitation

This Surah, like many Surahs in the final part (Juz’) of the Holy Quran, speaks of the themes of the Resurrection. In the beginning, it alludes to the horrific and world-shaking events of the end of the world and the onset of the Day of Judgment. In the second stage, it addresses the issue of the Resurrection and the accounting of the deeds of the righteous and the wicked, along with their ultimate fates. In the third stage, it discusses a portion of the actions and beliefs that lead to divine punishment and retribution. In the fourth stage, after mentioning several oaths, it refers to the stages of man's journey through the path of life in this world and the Hereafter. Finally, in the fifth stage, it once again speaks of good and evil deeds and their reward and punishment.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Inshiqaq, Allah will protect him from having his record of deeds given to him from behind his back on the Day of Judgment!" 1 And in the book Thawab al-A'mal, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that: "Whoever recites these two Surahs (Surah Al-Infitar and Surah Al-Inshiqaq) and keeps them before his eyes in his obligatory and recommended prayers, Allah will grant him his desires, and nothing shall intervene between him and Allah; he will continuously look upon the (grace of) Allah, and Allah shall look upon him, until the accounting of the people is finished." [2]

1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 458. Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 26, p. 294. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 536.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ (1) وَ أَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَ حُقَّتْ (2) وَ إِذَا الْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ (3) وَ أَلْقَتْ ما فِيها وَ تَخَلَّتْ (4) وَ أَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَ حُقَّتْ (5) يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ (6) فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ (7) فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً (8) وَ يَنْقَلِبُ إِلى أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً (9)

Commentary

A Toilsome Struggle Toward Absolute Perfection

As we mentioned in the description of the Surah's content, the beginning of this Surah also refers to the great and wondrous events of the end of the world, stating:

"When the sky is rent asunder" (and the celestial bodies and spheres are shattered, and the order of the stars is disrupted) (إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ) 1. This is similar to what appears in Surah Al-Infitar, which says: إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ وَ إِذَا الْكَواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ: "When the sky is cleft asunder, and when the stars are scattered" (Al-Infitar 1-2).

This is the announcement of the end of the world and its destruction and annihilation.

Then it adds: "And obeys its Lord, as it must" (وَ أَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَ حُقَّتْ). Lest it be imagined that the sky, with all its grandeur, offers the slightest resistance to this divine command; it is like an obedient and submissive servant who is completely surrendered to this decree.

"أَذِنَتْ" is originally derived from "أُذُن" (ear), meaning to listen and hear, and here it is a metaphor for obeying the command and submission.

"حُقَّتْ" is from the root "حَقّ", meaning worthy and deserving.

How can it not be submissive when the grace of existence reaches it moment by moment from Allah, and if this connection were severed for a single instant, it would shatter and perish?

Indeed, the heaven and the earth were not only obedient to the command of Truth at the beginning of creation—according to Verse 11 of Surah Fussilat: "قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ" (They both said, 'We come willingly')—but they are also so at the end of their life.

Some have also said that the meaning of the phrase "حُقَّتْ" is that the horror and fear of the Resurrection are such that it is only fitting for the heavens to rend asunder.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In the next stage, it refers to the state of the "earth," saying: "And when the earth is spread out" (وَ إِذَا الْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ).

The mountains—as evidenced by numerous verses of the Quran—will be completely pulverized and removed, and all heights and depths will vanish. The earth will become flat, expanded, and ready for the presence of all servants on the scene, just as He says in Verses 105 to 107 of Surah Ta-Ha: وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالِ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً فَيَذَرُها قاعاً صَفْصَفاً لا تَرى فِيها عِوجاً وَ لا أَمْتاً: "They ask you about the mountains. Say: 'My Lord will blast them into dust, then leave the earth a leveled plain, in which you will see no depression or elevation!'" And such a great court, where the first and the last of creation are gathered, requires such a setting! Some have said the meaning of the verse is that Allah will make the current earth much more expansive on the Day of Judgment than it is today, so it may have more capacity for the gathering and resurrection of the creatures. 1

In the third stage, it adds: "And casts out what is within it and becomes empty" (وَ أَلْقَتْ ما فِيها وَ تَخَلَّتْ). The well-known view among commentators is that the meaning of the verse is that all the dead who rested within the soil and inside the graves will suddenly be cast out and clothed in the garment of life, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Zalzalah: وَ أَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها "And the earth throws out its burdens," or what appears in Surah Al-Nazi'at, Verses 13 and 14: فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ بِالسَّاهِرَةِ: "It will be but a single shout, and behold, they will be upon the surface of the earth!" Some commentators have said that in addition to humans, the minerals and hidden treasures within the earth will also be cast out.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that the molten materials within the earth will be completely ejected by horrific and terrifying earthquakes, filling all the lowlands, and then the interior of the earth will become empty and still.

Combining these meanings is also possible.

Following this, it adds again: "And obeys its Lord, as it must" (وَ أَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَ حُقَّتْ). These great events, which are accompanied by the complete submission of all beings, on one hand, signify the annihilation of this world—the annihilation of the earth, the sky, humans, and treasures—and on the other hand, they serve as evidence for the creation of a turning point in the universe and a new and fresh stage of existence.

From a third perspective, it is a sign of the power of the Great Allah over all things, especially over the issue of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment.

Indeed, when these events occur, man shall see the result of his good and evil deeds (and this is a phrase that is implied).

Then, addressing humanity, He clarifies their fate in the path they have ahead, saying: "O man! Indeed, you are laboring toward your Lord with [great] exertion, and you shall meet Him" (يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ).

"كَدْح" (Kadh) means a struggle and effort accompanied by pain and toil that leaves an effect on the body and soul; hence, a hardworking ox whose body shows the signs of labor is called "ثور فيه كدوح".

In the commentaries of Al-Kashshaf, Fakhr Razi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani, it is stated that this word originally means a scratch inflicted upon the skin, and for this reason, it has been applied to efforts that leave an effect on the human soul.

This verse points to a fundamental principle in the lives of all humans: that life is always mingled with toil, pain, and hardship, even if the goal is to attain the goods of this world, let alone if the goal is the Hereafter, eternal bliss, and proximity to the Lord. This is the nature of worldly life; even those who live in ultimate luxury are not exempt from pain, toil, and suffering.

The expression "meeting the Lord" here—whether it refers to meeting the scene of the Resurrection, which is the scene of His absolute sovereignty, or meeting His recompense, reward, and punishment, or meeting Him through internal witnessing—shows that this pain and toil will continue until that day, and will end only when the file of this world is closed and man meets his God with pure deeds.

Indeed, comfort without pain and toil is found only there.

Addressing "man," which includes all of mankind (with an emphasis on the humanity of man), expresses the truth that Allah created the necessary powers for this continuous divine movement within the existence of this noblest of creatures.

The emphasis on the title "رَبّ" (Lord) indicates that this effort and struggle is part of the program of Lordship, evolution, and the upbringing of man.

Indeed, we are all travelers who have packed our bags from the border of non-existence and stepped into the realm of existence; we are all wayfarers toward the station of His love.

Similar expressions appear in other verses of the Quran, such as: وَ أَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى: "And that to your Lord is the finality" (An-Najm 42), and also: وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ الْمَصِيرُ "And to Allah is the destination" (Fatir 18).

And other verses, all of which indicate this constant journey of the evolution of beings toward the Exalted Lord.

However, here humans are divided into two groups, as He says: "Then as for him who is given his record in his right hand" (فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ).

"He will soon be judged with an easy accounting" (فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً).

"And he will return to his people joyful" (وَ يَنْقَلِبُ إِلى أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً). These are the ones who move in the primary orbit of creation—the very orbit that Allah has designated for this human and his capital and powers—and their struggle and effort are always for God, and their endeavor and movement are toward God. There, their record of deeds is given to their right hand, which is a sign of the purity of their actions, the correctness of their faith, and salvation in the Resurrection, and a source of pride and honor before the people of the gathering.

When they stand at the scale of justice—a scale that weighs the smallest of weights—Allah makes the accounting easy for them, overlooks their lapses, and, for the sake of their faith and righteous deeds, transforms their evil deeds into good deeds.

Regarding what is meant by "easy accounting" (hisaban yasira), some have said: it means a smooth and easy reckoning where there is no strictness or scrutiny; He overlooks the sins and rewards the good deeds.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "There are three things which, if found in anyone, Allah will judge him with an easy accounting and admit him into Paradise by His mercy." They asked: "And what are they, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "That you give to the one who deprived you, you join with the one who cut you off, and you forgive the one who wronged you." 1 It is also understood from the narrations that the precision and strictness of the accounting on the Day of Judgment depend on the level of intellect and knowledge of humans, as Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام says: "Allah only scrutinizes the servants in the accounting on the Day of Judgment according to the intellect He gave them in the world!" [2]

1: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 537.

Various interpretations have been mentioned for "people" (ahl) in the above verses. Some have said: it refers to his believing spouse and children whom the believers meet in Paradise, and this itself is a great blessing—that a person sees his loved ones in Paradise and is beside them.

Others have taken "people" to mean the heavenly maidens (houris) designated for the believers, and some have interpreted it as the believing individuals who were dear to him in the world and were on his path.

Combining these meanings is also possible.

Key Points

1- A Miraculous Hadith

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding the interpretation of "إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ", it is narrated that he said: "The heavens shall separate from the galaxy!" 1 This profound Hadith is worthy of attention and is considered one of the scientific miracles; it unveils a truth that no scientist had reached at that time. Today's astronomers, through their astronomical observations with vast and powerful telescopes, have proven that the universe is a collection of galaxies, and each galaxy is a collection of solar systems and stars. For this reason, they call them "cities of stars." The famous Milky Way galaxy, which is visible to the eye, is a vast, disk-like collection of these systems and stars. One side of it is so far from us that we see its stars as a white cloud, but in reality, it is a collection of luminous points close to one another.

1: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 87; Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 329.

However, the side that is close to us has stars that are visible, and these are the very stars we see in the night sky. Thus, our solar system is part of this "galaxy" (majarrah).

According to the above narration, Ali عليه السلام says: On the threshold of the Resurrection, these stars that we observe will separate from the galaxy, and the order of all will be disrupted.

Who at that time knew that the stars we see are actually part of this Milky Way galaxy (majarrah), except for one whose heart was connected to the world of the Unseen and who drank from the fountainhead of Allah's knowledge?

2- The World is a House of Toils and Pains

The expression "laboring" (kadih) in the above verses, which refers to effort and struggle mingled with pain and toil—considering that the addressees are all humans—expresses the reality that the nature of life in this world is never free from difficulties, discomforts, and hardship at any stage. Whether these difficulties are physical and bodily, or spiritual and intellectual, or both, we see no one free from them.

In a very profound Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read: "Comfort was not created in the world nor for the people of the world; comfort was only created in Paradise and for the people of Paradise. Toil and hardship were created in the world and for the people of the world. No one is given a portion of it except that he is given twice as much greed, and whoever acquires more of the world becomes poorer in it! Because he becomes in need of people to protect his wealth, and in need of every tool among the tools of the world; thus, there is no comfort in the wealth of the world!"

Then the Imam عليه السلام said at the end of this Hadith: "Never do the friends of Allah toil in the world for the sake of the world; rather, they toil in the world for the sake of the Hereafter!" 1

1: Khisal al-Saduq, Vol. 1, Chapter: "The World and the Hereafter are like the two scales of a balance," Hadith 95.

Ayahs

وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ وَرَآءَ ظَهْرِهِۦ (١٠)
But as for him who is given his record from behind his back, (10)
فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا (١١)
he will pray for annihilation (11)
وَيَصْلَىٰ سَعِيرًا (١٢)
and enter the Blaze. (12)
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فِىٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا (١٣)
Indeed he used to be joyful among his folk, (13)
إِنَّهُۥ ظَنَّ أَن لَّن يَحُورَ (١٤)
and he thought that he would never return. (14)
بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ رَبَّهُۥ كَانَ بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا (١٥)
Yes, his Lord had been watching him. (15)

Commentary

Those Who Receive Their Record from Behind Their Backs Out of Shame!

Following the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the "Companions of the Right" (the believers whose records of deeds are given to their right hands), these verses speak of the disbelievers and criminals and the nature of their records of deeds. First, It states:

"But as for he who is given his record behind his back," (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ وَراءَ ظَهْرِهِ‌).

"He will soon cry out, 'Woe to me, I am destroyed!'" (فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا ثُبُوراً).

"And he will enter to burn in a blazing Fire." (وَ يَصْلى‌ سَعِيراً).

Regarding how their records of deeds are given from behind their backs, and how this verse is reconciled with other verses stating that their records are given to their left hands, commentators have mentioned various interpretations:

Some have said: Their right hands will be chained to their necks, and their records of deeds will be given to their left hands from behind their backs, which is a sign of humiliation, abasement, and shame.

Others have said: Both of their hands will be tied behind their backs like captives, and their records of deeds will be placed in their left hands.

Others have suggested: In light of verse 47 of Surah An-Nisa, which says: مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَطْمِسَ وُجُوهاً فَنَرُدَّها عَلى‌ أَدْبارِها ("...before We efface faces and turn them backward"), the faces of this group of criminals will be turned backward, and since they must personally read their records, the records are given to their left hands behind them.

However, it is more appropriate to say that the Companions of the Right, with pride, honor, and exultation, take their records in their right hands and call out: هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ ("Here! Read my record!") (Al-Haqqah: 19). But when the wicked criminals are given their records in their left hands, they hold their hands behind their backs out of shame and humiliation, so that this document of crime and disgrace might be less visible—though what use is that, for nothing can be hidden there.

The phrase "يَدْعُوا ثُبُوراً" refers to an expression Arabs use upon the occurrence of a dangerous event, crying out: "Wa Thubura!" meaning "Woe, I am destroyed!" (Note that "Thubur" means destruction).

But this sighing, wailing, and shouting will lead nowhere, and following it is "وَ يَصْلى‌ سَعِيراً," meaning he shall enter the blazing fire of Hell.

Then, explaining the cause of this grim fate, He says: "Indeed, he had been among his people joyful (in his disbelief and sin)." (إِنَّهُ كانَ فِي أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً). A joy mixed with arrogance, and an arrogance mixed with negligence and heedlessness of God; a joy that was a sign of intense attachment to the world and indifference to the world after death.

Naturally, joy and happiness are not inherently blameworthy or condemned; rather, a believer should be joyful by the grace of Allah and be cheerful and pleasant in social interactions. The joy that is blameworthy is that which makes a person heedless of the remembrance of Allah and causes them to drown in lusts.

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "Indeed, he thought that he would never return," and that there is no Resurrection! (إِنَّهُ ظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ يَحُورَ). In reality, the primary source of his misery was his corrupt belief and false assumption regarding the denial of the Resurrection. This very belief caused his arrogance and joy, distanced him from Allah, and immersed him in lusts, to the point that he mocked the call of the Prophets. When he returned to his family, he was happy and joyful because of this mockery. This same meaning appears in verse 31 of Surah Al-Mutaffifin: وَ إِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِمُ انْقَلَبُوا فَكِهِينَ ("And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting"). Similarly, in the story of "Qarun," the arrogant and heedless wealthy man, it is mentioned that the knowledgeable among the Children of Israel said to him: لا تَفْرَحْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْفَرِحِينَ ("Do not exult. Indeed, Allah does not like the exultant [with pride].") (Al-Qasas: 76).

"Lan Yahur" (will never return) is from the root "Haur" (rhyming with Ghaur), which originally means "to recur" or "to go back and forth," whether this movement is in action or in thought. Thus, this word is applied to the circulation of water in a pool or basin. "Mihwar" (axis) refers to the rod around which a wheel rotates. "Muhawarah" means the back-and-forth exchange of discussion, and "Hiwar" carries the same meaning, sometimes referring to the shouting that rises during a debate.

"Tahayyur" (bewilderment) is also the result of the mind going back and forth on an issue, which necessitates wandering in action.

Some believe the root of this word is "Abyssinian" (Ethiopic). It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "I did not know the meaning of this phrase in the Quran until I heard a desert Arab say to his young daughter: 'Huri!' meaning 'Return.'" [1] The term "Hawari" (Disciple) regarding the close companions of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام or the close associates of anyone—

[^1]: Al-Mufradat by Raghib; Tafsir Fakhr Razi; Abu al-Futuh Razi, and other books.

—is perhaps for this reason: that they frequently go back and forth to him.

Some have also derived it from the root "Haur" meaning "to wash and whiten," because they purified the hearts of the people from the rust of polytheism and sin. The "Huris" of Paradise are named so because they are fair-skinned or because the whites of their eyes are very clear.

Some have also said that applying this word to the Huris of Paradise is because they are so beautiful that the eye becomes bewildered (hairan) upon seeing them.

In any case, this word in the verse under discussion means "return" and "Resurrection." Furthermore, this verse shows that lack of faith in the Resurrection becomes the source of negligence, arrogance, and entanglement in various sins.

In the final verse under discussion, to negate their false beliefs, He says: "Yes! Indeed, his Lord was ever seeing of him." (بَلى‌ إِنَّ رَبَّهُ كانَ بِهِ بَصِيراً). He recorded and preserved all his deeds and reflected them in his record of deeds for the Day of Account.

The expression in this verse, like the previous verse "O mankind, indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with [great] exertion and will meet Him," can be considered a proof for the issue of the Resurrection. This is especially true since both verses emphasize the title "Rabb" (Lord), for the evolutionary journey of man toward the Lord can never be stopped by death, and the life of this world is too insignificant to be the goal of such a journey.

Furthermore, Allah being "Basir" (All-Seeing) regarding the actions of man, and the recording and preservation of them, must certainly be a prelude to accounting and recompense; otherwise, it would be purposeless.

Ayahs

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلشَّفَقِ (١٦)
I swear by the evening glow, (16)
وَٱلَّيْلِ وَمَا وَسَقَ (١٧)
by the night and what it is fraught with, (17)
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا ٱتَّسَقَ (١٨)
by the moon when it blooms full: (18)
لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ (١٩)
you will surely fare from stage to stage. (19)
فَمَا لَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠)
What is the matter with them that they will not believe, (20)
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لَا يَسْجُدُونَ ۩ (٢١)
and will not prostrate when the Quran is recited to them? (21)
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٢)
Indeed, the faithless impugn [the Apostle], (22)
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُوعُونَ (٢٣)
and Allah knows best what they keep to themselves. (23)
فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٤)
So inform them of a painful punishment, (24)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍۭ (٢٥)
excepting such as are faithful and do righteous deeds: for them there will be an everlasting reward. (25)

Commentary

You are constantly changing!

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the evolutionary journey of man toward Allah, these verses emphasize this matter and provide further explanation, stating: "So I swear by the twilight" (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالشَّفَقِ).

"And by the night and what it gathers [of scattered affairs]" (وَ اللَّيْلِ وَ ما وَسَقَ).

"And by the moon when it becomes full" and turns into a complete orb (وَ الْقَمَرِ إِذَا اتَّسَقَ).

That all of you shall surely move from one state to another (لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقاً عَنْ طَبَقٍ).

The word "La" (لا) in the phrase "فَلا أُقْسِمُ" (I swear not), as we have previously mentioned, is "extra" (za'idah) and used for emphasis. However, some have suggested it might be for "negation," meaning "I do not swear [by these things]," either because the matter is so obvious that it requires no oath, or because the matter is so important that it is beyond such oaths, or that the things sworn by are so significant that they deserve not to be used in an oath.

However, the first meaning (being extra and for emphasis) is the most appropriate of all.

"Shafaq" (twilight), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, is the blending of the light of day with the darkness of night. Therefore, the word "Ishfaq" is used to mean attention and care mixed with fear; for example, when a person cares for someone and simultaneously fears for them regarding certain events, this state is called "Ishfaq" and the person is called "Mushfiq."

Fakhr Razi, however, believes that the word "Shafaq" originally means "thinness" or "delicacy," which is why very thin clothing is called "Shafaq," and the state of "Shafaqat" (compassion) refers to the tenderness of the heart (though Raghib's view seems more accurate).

In any case, "Shafaq" here refers to that light mixed with darkness at the beginning of the night. Since at the start of the night, a faint redness first appears on the western horizon and then gives way to whiteness, there is a disagreement as to whether "Shafaq" refers to that redness or the whiteness. The well-known view among scholars and commentators is the first meaning, which is also emphasized in poetry, where twilight is likened to "Dima' al-Shuhada" (the blood of martyrs).

Some have chosen the second meaning, which seems very weak, especially since if we consider the linguistic root to be "thinness," it aligns better with that faint redness which is the "thin" light of the sun.

Regardless, since the appearance of "Shafaq" signals a state of profound transformation in the world and announces the end of day and the beginning of night—and additionally possesses a unique beauty and splendor, and above all, marks the time for the Maghrib prayer—Allah has sworn by it to urge everyone to reflect upon this beautiful celestial phenomenon.

As for the oath by the night, it is due to the many effects and secrets hidden within it, which we have discussed in detail previously [1]. The expression "ma wasaq" 2, considering that "wasq" means to gather scattered things [3], refers to the return of various animals, birds, and even humans to their homes and nests at nightfall, resulting in general peace and rest for living beings. This is considered one of the most important secrets and effects of the night, as we read in Surah Ghafir, verse 61: "Allah is He who made for you the night that you may rest therein."

[1]: Refer to Volume 16, page 146, under verses 71 to 73 of Surah Al-Qasas.

[3]: "Wasq" (on the pattern of ghadab) also means a camel-load or sixty "sa'", where each "sa'" is nearly three kilograms, also because of its gathered nature.

From this, it becomes clear that the expression "idha-ttasaq," which comes from the same root, also means to be gathered and organized, and here it refers to the perfection of the moon's light on the fourteenth night. We know that the moon in this state has an extraordinary splendor that draws all eyes toward it; its light illuminates the surface of the earth—a faint light that does not disturb the peace of the night, yet opens the way for night travelers. Therefore, it is one of the great signs of Allah, and for this reason, He has sworn by it.

It is noteworthy that all four subjects sworn by in these verses (twilight, night, the creatures the night gathers, and the moon in its full state) are interrelated and complementary, forming a beautiful and cohesive set that stimulates human thought to reflect on the great power of creation and to become more acquainted with the issue of the Resurrection and Allah's power over it through these rapid transformations.

Interestingly, these oaths refer to states and changes that occur sequentially in the world of creation: the sun hides its face, the twilight (the remains of its light) appears, living creatures quickly return to their nests, and the moon rises in its full state (note that the full moon of the fourteenth night rises at the very beginning of the night). These oaths serve as a prelude to the sentence: "You shall surely move from one state to another" (لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقاً عَنْ طَبَقٍ), which describes the various stages man experiences one after another in the course of his life.

Various interpretations have been mentioned for this sentence, including:

  1. It refers to the various states man experiences on his path of toil and hardship toward Allah and Absolute Perfection: first the world, then the realm of Barzakh, then the Resurrection and its various states (note that "tabaq" comes from the root "mutabaqah," meaning placing one thing above another, and it also refers to the stages man traverses in his ascending journey).

  2. It refers to the stages man passes through from the time he is a drop of semen until the moment of death, which some have numbered as thirty-seven states!

  3. It refers to the various conditions man undergoes in worldly life: health and sickness, sorrow and joy, grief and happiness, hardship and ease, peace and war.

  4. It refers to the various states and hardships man faces on the Day of Judgment until the reckoning is finished and everyone goes toward the result of their deeds, whether Paradise or Hell.

  5. It refers to the states that occurred in previous nations; meaning the same bitter and sweet events, and the types of denials and rejections by opponents, will also occur in this Ummah. This meaning is narrated in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام.

Of course, there is no obstacle to combining these interpretations, and the verse may be referring to all these transformations, changes, and stages that man traverses in the course of his life.

Some have considered the addressee in this verse to be the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله personally, seeing the verse as a reference to the heavens the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله passed through on the night of Mi'raj. However, given that the "ba" in "latarkabunna" carries a dammah (indicating a plural subject), this interpretation is not suitable, especially since the previous verses addressed all of humanity.

In any case, the occurrence of these states and man's lack of stability in one condition is, on one hand, proof of his being a created being in need of a Creator—since every changing thing is originated (hadith), and every originated thing requires a Creator—and on the other hand, it is proof of the transience of this world. Thirdly, it is a sign of man's continuous movement toward the Essence of the Lord and the matter of the Resurrection, as stated in the previous verses: "O man! Verily you are toiling toward your Lord with a [great] toil, and you shall meet Him."

Then, as a general conclusion from the previous discussions, it asks: "So what is the matter with them that they do not believe?" (فَما لَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Despite the fact that the proofs of truth are clear and manifest—both the proofs of Monotheism and the knowledge of God, and the proofs of the Resurrection; both the "horizonal" signs (afaqi) hidden in the creation of night and day, moon and sun, light and darkness, the rising and setting of the sun, the twilight, the darkness of night, and the perfection of the moon's light; and the "internal" signs (anfusi) from the moment the semen settles in the womb and passes through various stages one after another until it reaches its peak of perfection in the fetal world, then traverses other stages from birth until death—with these clear signs, why do they not believe?!

Then, moving from the "Book of Creation" (Takwin) to the "Written Book" (Tadwin), it adds: "And when the Quran is recited to them, they do not prostrate?" (وَ إِذا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقُرْآنُ لا يَسْجُدُونَ).

A Quran that, like the sun, is its own proof; the light of miracle shines from its various aspects, and its content is a clear proof that it is derived from the source of revelation. A Quran that any impartial observer knows could not be the product of a human brain, especially a human who was unschooled and raised in a dark environment full of superstitions.

The meaning of "prostration" (sajdah) here is humility, submission, and obedience [1], and the well-known prostration where the forehead is placed on the ground is one of the instances of this general concept. Perhaps for this reason, it is mentioned in some narrations that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited these verses, he prostrated.

Of course, according to the well-known fatwas of the jurists of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, this prostration is among the recommended (mustahabb) prostrations of the Quran, while the four Sunni schools of thought all consider prostration mandatory (wajib) upon reciting this verse, except for Malik, who believes the prostration should be performed after the end of the Surah 2.

In the next verse, it adds: "Nay, those who disbelieve persist in denying [the divine verses and the Resurrection]" (بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُكَذِّبُونَ).

The use of the "present/continuous verb" (mudari') here, which usually denotes continuity, is evidence that they were persistent in their denials—a persistence rooted in a spirit of obstinacy and enmity. It was a denial that did not stem from any inadequacy in the proofs of truth, but rather from prejudice, blind following of ancestors, preservation of material interests, and the pursuit of freedom to satisfy satanic desires.

Then, in a threatening tone, it says: "And Allah knows best what they keep hidden [within their hearts]" (وَ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُوعُونَ).

Allah is well aware of their intentions, goals, and the motives that cause these continuous denials, no matter how much they try to cover them up, and He will ultimately punish them for all of it.

"Yu'un" comes from the root "wi'a'," meaning a vessel, as mentioned in the famous statement of Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha: "Indeed, these hearts are vessels, and the best of them is the one that preserves and holds the most!"

And in the next verse, it says: "So give them tidings of a painful punishment" (فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

The use of the term "bashara" (glad tidings), which is usually used for good news, is a form of sarcasm and reproach here. This is while the believers are truly given glad tidings of the vast blessings of Paradise, so that the deniers destined for Hell may fall into regret and sorrow.

In the final verse of this Surah, as an exception, it refers once more to the fate of the righteous believers, stating: "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds; for them is a reward constant, uninterrupted, and free from any reduction or obligation" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ).

"Mamnun" from the root "mann" means both "interruption" and "reduction," as well as "obligation" (the word manun meaning death also comes from this root). It is possible that all these meanings are combined here, because the blessings of the Hereafter—unlike the blessings of this world which are unstable, subject to reduction, and often mixed with undesirable side effects or the obligation (mann) of this or that person—have no obligation, reduction, cessation, or undesirable effects. They are eternal, inexhaustible, and free from any unpleasantness or obligation.

Whether this "exception" is "connected" (muttasil) or "disconnected" (munqati') is a matter of debate among commentators. Some have suggested it is disconnected, meaning it leaves the description of the disbelievers mentioned in the previous verses and moves to the eternal reward of the believers.

However, it is more appropriate for the exception to be connected, with the goal of opening the door of return for the disbelievers, saying: this painful punishment will certainly be removed from those who repent, believe, and perform righteous deeds, and He will give them a permanent and inexhaustible reward.

Key Points

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan derives two points from the final verses of this Surah: first, the principle of free will and freedom of choice, because blaming someone for not prostrating or not believing would be unjust for the Wise Allah if the individuals were compelled (forced). The phrase "So what is the matter with them that they do not believe, and when the Quran is recited to them, they do not prostrate?" is clear evidence for the issue of free will. Secondly, the blame for not prostrating is evidence that the disbelievers are obligated to the branches of religion (furu') just as they are obligated to the principles (usul). (This argument holds if "prostration" in the verse refers to the specific prostration of prayer, or at least has a broad meaning that includes it).

O Allah! On that day when everyone appears in Your court of justice, make the reckoning easy for us. O Lord! In this path where all servants are moving toward You, help us in traversing the Straight Path. O God! We have bowed our heads in submission before Your Noble Quran; grant us all the success to act upon it.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Inshiqaq.
